slides.heading: You do not need to be a computer person
slides.body:
You do not need to be a "computer person" to understand how systems work.

There is no requirement to already know things, no expectation to move fast, and no need to prove anything. What matters is simple: you are curious about how this works beneath the surface.

That curiosity is enough.

This is not about becoming a "power user" or fitting into a category. It is about understanding what is happening inside a machine you already use every day.

So we will begin from there, looking at things directly, without assumptions.

slides.heading: Why Linux Exists
slides.body:
Most operating systems are designed like closed machines. You can use them, but you cannot see how they work internally, modify them freely, or control their behavior completely.

Linux comes from a different idea: your system should be transparent and modifiable.

It also runs across a wide range of systems, from embedded devices to large-scale servers. Its design prioritizes control and flexibility over abstraction.
bullets: You can inspect how it works
You are not restricted by a single vendor's decisions

slides.heading: What Linux Actually Is
slides.body:
Linux is not the interface you see or the applications you run.

At its core, it is a kernel, a layer that sits between hardware and software.

It is responsible for managing memory, handling processes, coordinating input and output, and allowing software to interact with hardware safely.

Everything else, including desktop environments, tools, and applications, is built on top of this layer.
bullets: Lets software talk to hardware safely

slides.heading: How It Started
slides.body:
In 1991, Linus Torvalds began writing a kernel as a personal project while studying in Finland.

He shared it publicly, and others began contributing. Over time, it evolved into a collaborative system maintained by developers across the world.

Today, it powers a significant portion of global infrastructure, from servers to supercomputers.
bullets: Started as a personal kernel project in 1991
